source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A flyball governor consists of two masses $m$ connected to arms of length $l$ and a mass $M$ as shown in Fig. 2.68. The assembly is constrained to rotate around a shaft on which the mass $M$ can slide up and down without friction. Neglect the mass of the arms, air friction, and assume that the diameter of the mass $M$ is small. Suppose the shaft is now allowed to rotate freely. Does the frequency of small oscillation change? If so, calculate the new value.

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A flyball governor consists of two masses $m$ connected to arms of length $l$ and a mass $M$ as shown in Fig. 2.68. The assembly is constrained to rotate around a shaft on which the mass $M$ can slide up and down without friction. Neglect the mass of the arms, air friction, and assume that the diameter of the mass $M$ is small. Suppose the shaft is now allowed to rotate freely. Does the frequency of small oscillation change? If so, calculate the new value.
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object representation rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: When the shaft is free to rotate, using the Lagrangian with $\omega=\dot{\varphi}$ leads to the conserved quantity $\dot{\varphi}\sin^{2}\theta=c$. The subsequent Lagrange equations yield, at equilibrium ($\ddot{\theta}=0$, $\dot{\theta}=0$, $\theta=\theta_{0}$), the condition $$mc^{2}\frac{\cos\theta_{0}}{\sin^{3}\theta_{0}}=(m+M)g\sin\theta_{0}.$$ For small oscillations about $\theta_{0}$ with $\theta=\theta_{0}+\theta'$ and appropriate linearizations, the equation of motion becomes $$ (m+2M\sin^{2}\theta_{0})l\ddot{\theta}'+(m+M)g\frac{(1+3\cos^{2}\theta_{0})}{\cos\theta_{0}}\theta'=0. $$ Hence, the oscillation frequency is $$f=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(m+M)g(1+3\cos^{2}\theta_{0})}{(m+2M\sin^{2}\theta_{0})l\cos\theta_{0}}}.$$

problem_main_record.reasoning_chain: When the shaft is free to rotate, using the Lagrangian with $\omega=\dot{\varphi}$ leads to the conserved quantity $\dot{\varphi}\sin^{2}\theta=c$. The subsequent Lagrange equations yield, at equilibrium ($\ddot{\theta}=0$, $\dot{\theta}=0$, $\theta=\theta_{0}$), the condition $$mc^{2}\frac{\cos\theta_{0}}{\sin^{3}\theta_{0}}=(m+M)g\sin\theta_{0}.$$ For small oscillations about $\theta_{0}$ with $\theta=\theta_{0}+\theta'$ and appropriate linearizations, the equation of motion becomes $$ (m+2M\sin^{2}\theta_{0})l\ddot{\theta}'+(m+M)g\frac{(1+3\cos^{2}\theta_{0})}{\cos\theta_{0}}\theta'=0. $$ Hence, the oscillation frequency is $$f=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(m+M)g(1+3\cos^{2}\theta_{0})}{(m+2M\sin^{2}\theta_{0})l\cos\theta_{0}}}.$$
